what_this_pass_proved: The fixed-set-multi-select interaction rule is learnable: players can discover that trucks disappear from the yard when dispatched and return when tapped on the board, without written instructions.

The solvability guarantee works in practice: every generated demand is reachable by at least one subset of the available trucks. No unsolvable boards were produced after getSolvableDemands() was introduced.
The overshoot block (cannot add a truck whose capacity would exceed demand) teaches constraint reasoning through behavior, not text — the truck shake is readable as 'this won't fit'.
The game is architecturally distinct from Bakery Rush: it is resource_allocation_under_constraints, not additive_accumulation. Players must find the right combination, not accumulate a total.
Urgency (HIGH/MED/LOW) is mechanically meaningful when backed by a points multiplier — players notice the badge changes their score.
open_questions: Does a first-time player understand WHY some demands require a specific combination — i.e., does the subset-sum constraint feel like a puzzle or like an arbitrary rule?
Is 25 seconds at Level 1 enough time for a new player to think through combinations, or does the timer add pressure before comprehension is established?
Do players discover the board-tap return mechanic without prompting, or do they assume dispatched trucks are permanently gone until the round resets?
Level 4 removes the Rescue Unit from available trucks, narrowing the effective solvable demand range below what the declared range [8,18] implies. Is this noticeable as a difficulty spike?

bugs_fixed.description: generateIncident() picked random integers from the demand range without checking subset-sum feasibility. Targets 4 and 6 with Level 1 trucks [5,3,2] were unreachable. Fixed with getSolvableDemands() — bitmask enumeration of all non-empty subsets filtered to range.
Level advancement threshold check used prevScore+pts >= threshold inside a setState updater, double-counting pts because prevScore was already post-update. Fixed to prevScore >= threshold.

Urgency badge (🔴 HIGH / 🟡 MED / 🟢 LOW) was purely decorative — identical points and time limits regardless of urgency. Fixed: HIGH=1.5×, MED=1.0×, LOW=0.8× multiplier on base points. Success overlay names the bonus.

Selected trucks remained visible in the yard with a checkmark overlay after dispatch. Players could not distinguish 'in yard' from 'queued on board'. Fixed: dispatched trucks removed from TruckYard; board chips are tappable to return them.

known_limits: Single demand axis (fire capacity only). Multi-attribute matching (fire + rescue) is architecturally designed for but not yet built.
Urgency is randomly assigned per incident — not tied to incident type or location. A 'House Fire' can be HIGH priority while a 'Chemical Spill' is LOW.
Timer starts immediately on incident spawn — no free-exploration window for first-time players to read the available trucks before pressure begins.
Level 4 removes the Rescue Unit, silently narrowing the effective solvable demand range. Players see the declared range [8,18] but the actual solvable range is smaller.
No hint or partial-credit mechanism for players who exhaust all combinations without finding the right one.

auditor_results.notes: Auditor run against timeLimit [25,20,18,15,12] and demandRangeMax [8,12,15,18,20]. Steps within thresholds on both parameters. FIRE_DISPATCH_PARAMETER_CONFIGS thresholds are estimated defaults — not yet validated against a prototype spec artifact. No Bakery-equivalent proof case run yet for Fire Dispatch.

next_pass_first_obligation: Add a second demand axis (Rescue) so that each incident requires matching both fire capacity and rescue capacity simultaneously, and prove that two-attribute reasoning is learnable at Level 1 without the cognitive load becoming prohibitive.
